data.title: Contractor indicted in leak case tied to search of Washington Post reporter's home
data.description:
A government contractor who allegedly leaked classified information in a case linked to the search of a Washington Post journalist’s home was indicted by a grand jury on Thursday. 

Aurelio Luis Perez-Lugones is charged with five counts of unlawfully transmitting and one count of unlawfully retaining classified national defense information, the Justice Department (DOJ) said. 

Perez-Lugones allegedly “repeatedly accessed classified reports” and removed them from his sensitive compartmented information facility (SCIF) to then share with a reporter, according to the DOJ’s release. 

“Illegally disclosing classified defense information is a grave crime against America that puts both our national security and the lives of our military heroes at risk,” Attorney General Pam Bondi said in the release. “This Department of Justice will remain ever-vigilant in protecting the integrity of America’s classified intelligence.”

The DOJ also said that Perez-Lugones had previously been charged with retaining national defense information. 

The indictment follows the FBI’s search of Post reporter Hannah Natanson’s Virginia home. Federal agents took several devices, including two phones, two laptops, a recorder, a portable hard drive and a Garmin watch, according to the newspaper.

Natanson has reported on the Trump administration’s efforts to cut down on government spending by shrinking the federal workforce. 

On Wednesday, the Post submitted a request to a judge to order the return of these materials. 

The newspaper wrote in its request that this seizure “violates the Constitution’s protections for free speech and a free press and should not be allowed to stand.”

“It is a prior restraint and a violation of the reporter’s privilege that flouts the First Amendment and ignores federal statutory safeguards for journalists,” the Post wrote. “The seizure chills speech, cripples reporting, and inflicts irreparable harm every day the government keeps its hands on protected material.”

data.title: Trump's overreaction to charges of fraud is harming thousands — and that's the goal
data.description:
Earlier this month, there was rare, broad consensus at a House subcommittee hearing that child care is critically important to children, families, employers and our economy — and that the system we have right now does not meet those stakeholders’ needs.  

I testified that we can’t build a system where families can find and afford quality child care, and early educators are well-compensated for skillfully caring for our youngest children, without robust public investments.

Unfortunately, the administration’s recent actions freezing federal child care funding threaten to further destabilize our fragile and underfunded system.

In response to allegations of fraud from a right-wing influencer who visited child care providers in Minnesota demanding to know “where is the money going,” the Trump administration froze federal child care funds to the entire state of Minnesota. In addition, the administration froze child care and human services funding to California, New York, Illinois and Colorado. Fortunately, a federal court temporarily blocked the administration’s actions in all five states.  

But the administration wasn’t finished.  

It then announced that it would require all states to provide additional information in order to receive federal child care funds — what it termed “defend the spend.” The administration also proposed to eliminate portions of a regulation that capped child care  payments for families and made payments more predictable and stable for providers. What’s more, President Trump sent ICE agents to Minnesota and other states, with violent and tragic results, and announced that the Department of Justice will create a new position charged with investigating fraud.  

As a parent, I can tell you the administration’s attack on our already precarious child care system breaks my heart. And as an expert on child care and early learning, I know that such an outsized response will cause serious harm to parents, providers and communities.  

If California, Colorado, Illinois, Minnesota and New York do not receive federal child care and social services funding, about 300,000 children could be directly impacted. Thousands of parents would lose the child care they need to work or look for work. Businesses would lose productivity and revenues. Child care providers may go out of business.  And even though the funding freeze has been enjoined, more “concerned citizens” have started  pounding on child care providers’ doors in Minnesota, and reportedly in Ohio, Washington state and Maine. 

To be sure, for the moment, a court has forbidden the administration to withhold funds. But, in another sense, harm has already been done. Fear and uncertainty, among parents, providers and communities, is rampant. Families don’t know if they will be able to take their children to child care. Providers don’t know if they will be able to pay rent and make payroll.

A life has been lost. An army of vigilantes feels it has been given license to terrorize child care providers, early educators and the children in their care. And communities are bracing for worse to come.   

It’s telling that while the administration responded to unproven allegations from a conservative YouTuber with lightning speed, it has done nothing over the past year to make the child care system work any better for families, providers or teachers. In fact, it’s taken steps at every turn to make the child-care crisis worse. It’s almost as though the administration’s true goals have nothing to do with building a system that works for everyone, and everything to do with punishing political opponents, demonizing immigrants, and cutting basic needs programs that struggling families rely on.  

We need to strengthen and build the child-care system we all need and deserve — not harm thousands of children, families, providers and communities by threatening to freeze funding.

Amy K. Matsui, JD, is vice president for child care and income security at the National Women’s Law Center, and a Public Voices Fellow of the OpEd Project in Partnership with the National Black Child Development Institute.

data.title: Watch live: Vance, Johnson speak at March for Life rally
data.description:
Vice President Vance and Speaker Mike Johnson (R-La.) are scheduled to speak Friday at the annual March for Life rally in Washington D.C.

Vance and President Trump spoke to anti-abortion advocates at last year’s event. It has been held every year since 1974.

Watch Friday’s remarks, scheduled to begin at 12 p.m. EST, in the player above.

data.title: Carney pushes back on Trump remarks about Canada: 'The choice is up to us'
data.description:
Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney on Thursday praised his country, saying “the choice is up to us” when it comes to Canada’s future, after President Trump said Canada “lives because of the U.S.”

“Canada and the United States have built a remarkable partnership, in the economy, in security, and in rich cultural exchange,” Carney wrote on the social platform X. “But Canada doesn’t ‘live because of the United States.’ Canada thrives because we are Canadian.”

Carney offered the remarks upon his return from the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, where he made headlines with a speech that sought to stand up for “middle powers” who he said must “act together because if we’re not at the table, we’re on the menu.” The audience gave Carney a standing ovation for the speech, which highlighted ruptures in the post-WWII world order prompted by President Trump without naming him.

On Wednesday, Trump responded to Carney, saying Canada saying Canada “should be grateful” to the United States because of the security it provides.

“I watched your prime minister yesterday, he wasn’t so grateful,” Trump told the audience in Davos.

“But they should be grateful to us, Canada,” the president added. “Canada lives because of the United States. Remember that, Mark, before you make your statements.”

In his latest remarks, Carney may have been offering a response of sorts to those remarks from Trump. Carney said that “we are masters in our home, this is our own country, it’s our future, the choice is up to us.”

On Friday, Trump withdrew Carney’s invitation to join his Board of Peace to oversee the rehabilitation of the Gaza Strip. The board’s formation is part of Trump’s 20-point peace plan after Israel and Hamas agreed to a ceasefire to the two-year-long conflict that devastated Gaza and left tens of thousands of Palestinians dead.

“Dear Prime Minister Carney,” Trump wrote on Truth Social. “Please let this Letter serve to represent that the Board of Peace is withdrawing its invitation to you regarding Canada’s joining, what will be, the most prestigious Board of Leaders ever assembled, at any time.”

The Hill has reached out to the White House and Carney’s office for comment on Trump’s Friday post.

Carney received the invitation to join the board last week, Reuters reported. He questioned the Board of Peace’s $1 billion membership fee, telling reporters on Sunday that Canada “wants money to have maximum impact.”

“We still do not have unimpeded aid flows, humanitarian aid flows at scale to the people in Gaza,” he said. “That is a precondition for moving forward on this.”

The Associated Press contributed.

data.title: Trump appeases Putin while invading US cities
data.description:
Ukrainians are freezing and dying in the dark this winter as Russian missiles and drones relentlessly pound their power plants and other civilian targets. You’d expect Americans, who fought for eight long years to win their independence from another colonial power, would side instinctively with Ukraine.

And they do. Most — now including a majority of Republicans — favor sending more U.S. military assistance to Kyiv. Yet President Trump seems less moved than peeved by Ukraine’s stubborn resistance to Russia’s savage war of conquest and refuses its defenders weapons they desperately need to even the odds.

It seems the president values his unrequited man-crush on Russian dictator Vladimir Putin more than the trivial matter of Ukrainians’ freedom. This week, Trump made a mockery of his own “Board of Peace” for Gaza by inviting the Kremlin warlord to join.  

Perhaps to impress his bellicose pal, Trump has turned to war. He’s attacked Venezuela. He threatened to bomb Iran again if it doesn’t stop killing protesters. And in a fit of pique over not winning a Nobel Peace Prize, he vowed to seize Greenland by force before backing off in a bizarre speech to world leaders Wednesday in Davos. 

That would have shattered the NATO alliance, trading hundreds of millions of prosperous and once-friendly Europeans for a frozen island with around 57,000 inhabitants. Such a deal! 

Trump also is radiating belligerence on the home front. He’s threatening to send U.S. troops to Minnesota to put down a phantom “insurrection” against his administration’s ugly mass deportation campaign. 

What’s really happening is that Minnesotans are protesting the militarized tactics of ICE agents, who are mistreating illegal immigrants and U.S. citizens alike. No doubt they’re also wondering why our “America First” president shows more respect for the rights of Iranian protesters than American protesters. 

Trump has dispatched more than 3,000 federal agents to Minnesota, essentially putting the Twin Cities under federal occupation. Why? 

It’s not because Minnesota is overrun by “criminal aliens.” It is home to less than 100,000 living in the country illegally, compared to more than 1 million in places like Texas, Florida and California.

The state has given shelter to many Somali refugees, for whom Trump harbors special animus. He’s seized on a child care fraud scandal involving some Somalis as a pretext for federal intervention.

Trump also wants to punish blue metros that have designated themselves “sanctuary cities” to signal non-cooperation with harsh enforcement of U.S. immigration laws. It is mostly progressive political Kabuki, since Minneapolis officials aren’t running an Underground Railroad for illegal migrants.

Immigration and Custom Enforcement’s methods are un-American, more fitting for a police state than a free society. Agents dress in paramilitary garb, wear masks, drive in unmarked cars and don’t bother to produce warrants as they snatch suspects from schools, vehicles, workplaces and homes. 

Their overbearing conduct inevitably draws demonstrators, against whom ICE agents deploy tear gas, pepper spray and stun grandees. On Jan. 7, an ICE agent shot and killed a 37-year-old Minneapolis mother, Renee Nicole Good. She was a protester, but any “threat” she posed to armed ICE agents was of their own making. 

When the police shoot a civilian, officials normally urge calm and launch a use-of-force investigation. Instead, Trump rushed to social media to blame the victim, then falsely asserted in a New York Times interview that Good ran over an ICE agent. Homeland Security chief Kristi Noem chimed in, calling Good a “domestic terrorist.”  

Trump also is unleashing “lawfare” on Minnesotans. The U.S. Justice Department ordered federal prosecutors in Minnesota to probe Good and her partner’s ties to citizen groups monitoring ICE’s conduct. Six prosecutors promptly resigned rather than abet this blatant bid to criminalize dissent.

The Justice Department has launched an equally farcical investigation into Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) and Minneapolis Mayor Jacob Frey (D) for obstructing federal agents, simply because they criticized ICE’s strongarm tactics. 

“The only person not being investigated for the shooting of Renee Good is the federal agent who shot her,” Walz notes. (ICE agents also shot a second person in Minneapolis last week in his home, though not fatally.)  

Apart from hardcore Republicans, the public isn’t buying Trump’s gaslighting. Polls show Americans increasingly disapprove of ICE’s trigger-happy tactics and most say that Good’s slaying was unjustified.  

Using hyperbolic social media videos to recruit young men for battle against an alien invasion of “the worst of the worst,” Trump and his lackeys are turning an essential federal law enforcement agency into a MAGA goon squad. 

If we had one, a functioning Congress would act swiftly to decontaminate and depoliticize ICE. But Republicans are terrified of crossing Trump, even as Americans again recoil — as they did during his first term — from his dehumanizing immigration policies. 

And a decent president, if we had one, would stand up to blood-soaked tyrants like Putin rather than treating fellow Americans as enemies.   

Will Marshall is founder and president of Progressive Policy Institute.

data.title: Trump not 'huge fan' of housing plan that would open 401(k)s for down payments
data.description:
President Trump said late Thursday he does not support his administration’s proposal to allow people to tap into their 401(k)s in order to secure down payments for homes. 

“I’m not a huge fan of it. Other people like it. They’re talking about taking money out to put a deposit down on a home. And one of the reasons I don’t like it is that their 401(k)s are doing so well. 401(k)s are up 80, 90 percent in some cases. And you know, you’re talking about a lot of people,” Trump told reporters on Air Force One en route to Washington. 

 “I like keeping their 401(k)s in great shape. I like keeping their 401(k)s. And I’m not a huge fan of that, putting down a deposit,” he continued. 

Trump’s comments appear to mark a reversal on the administration’s stance on the matter after his aides said they were in the process of finalizing a plan that would allow people to take money from their 401(k)s to use for a down payment.

Last week, National Economic Director Kevin Hassett touted the proposal during a Fox Business interview. 

“We’re going to allow people to take money out of their 401(k)s and use that for a down payment,” Hassett told the network. “We’re still talking about the mechanics of it.” 

The proposal is an example of how Trump is taking on the issue of affordability as Republicans grapple with the issue ahead of the midterms. 

Trump promoted policies encouraging homeownership in his speech to the World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland this week. 

“Homes are built for people, not for corporations,” the president said. “It’s just not fair to the public. They are not able to buy a house.”

data.title: Trump warns 'armada' heading to Iran: 'Maybe we won't have to use it'
data.description:
President Trump warned late Thursday that the U.S. is sending an “armada” toward Iran amid nationwide demonstrations against the regime, but questioned whether the U.S. would actually take any action against Tehran. 

“We have a big flotilla going in that direction,” Trump told reporters on AirForce One en route to Washington from Davos, Switzerland. “We have a big force going toward Iran. I’d rather not see anything happen but we’re watching them very closely.” 

Trump went on to say he stopped Iran’s leadership from hanging 837 people on Thursday. The president has previously threatened to take action against Iran if they execute protesters. 

“I said if you hang those people, you’re going to be hit harder than you’ve ever been hit,” the president said. “They actually said they cancelled it. They didn’t postpone it, they cancelled it. So that was a good sign.” 

“But we have an armada. We have a massive fleet heading in that direction and maybe we won’t have to use it, we’ll see,” he said. 

Iran’s top prosecutor on Friday called Trump’s repeated claims that he halted hangings there “completely false.”

Weeks of antigovernment protests in Iran have resulted in more than 3,300 deaths following clashes with Iran’s security forces.

Ayatollah Ali Khamenei has accused the U.S. of plotting against the Iranian government and backing the protests, claiming the U.S. was attempting to “devour Iran.”

“We do not intend to lead the country toward war,” Khamenei wrote in one of several posts on the social platform X on Saturday. “However, we will not just let go of the criminals inside the country either. Worse than the internal criminals are the international criminals! We will not let go of them either.”

data.title: Whole Hog Politics: Democrats find 'reform' a better rejoinder than 'democracy'
data.description:
[Watch Whole Hog Politics live: Join our live online audience on Jan. 30 and ask your questions to Chris Stirewalt and host Bill Sammon. Click here to join us!] 

Close

Thank you for signing up!

Subscribe to more newsletters here

The latest in politics and policy. Direct to your inbox. Sign up for the Whole Hog Politics newsletter

Two seemingly disparate but very much related stories played out in politics this week. One, the whole world watched. The other happened quietly and mostly behind closed doors. 

Nobody missed President Trump’s Greenlandic opera, which took us from the potential end of NATO to “Kumbaya” in the span of about 24 hours once credit and investment markets had their say about the president’s Arctic brinkmanship. 

The narrative arc among Trump officials and core supporters was a familiar one: 1) He’s only trolling. 2) He’s actually raising valid points. 3) Actually, he’s deadly serious and very much correct. 4) Art of the Deal, suckers! 

Step three is the essential pivot. For Trump to engage in his preferred brand of negotiations, his threat to do something genuinely awful and self-destructive has to be credible. But the more times he pulls these U-turns, the harder it is to convince people he really is so reckless. That’s particularly true of the people in his administration and his supporters, whose fear of an actual catastrophe is an essential ingredient. 

Threatening to burn down your own house in order to get your neighbor to move a fence off the property line doesn’t work if your family tells everybody you’re just upset and would happily find a win-win solution. This is the TACO conundrum. In order to keep bluffing, a gambler needs to continually raise the stakes. For low-probability outcomes to provide motivation to opponents, the consequences must be high — maybe even existential. 

As George Costanza put it: “It’s not a lie if you believe it.” That’s true for Trump himself, whose capacity for the construction of alternate realities is profound, but also for his own people. And getting people who know him, like him and want him to succeed to believe he might be a monster requires Trump to absolutely commit to the bit. And that brings us back to Kurt Vonnegut’s famous line: “We are what we pretend to be, so we must be careful what we pretend to be.” 

All of this bluffing and bullying has consequences for Trump and for the people in his faction. Climbing down only gets harder. That means the tactics by his political allies and subordinates that often worked in constraining Trump before — “We can’t do X, but how about Y?” — are becoming less and less effective. Add in the purge (and self-deportation) of old-fashioned, rule-of-law conservatives from MAGA and you have a recipe for a very wild midterm election year. 

Some Democrats say that the sudden, irrational fixation on Greenland was a ploy to distract from other, more damaging news stories, particularly Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) tactics, the ongoing questions about the future of Venezuela as a U.S. client state and the failure to release the Epstein files as required by law. In this way, MAGA and anti-MAGA are alike: They see Trump playing four-dimensional chess when simpler solutions are available.   

It’s easier to explain Greenland as the knock-on to all of the Venezuela flexing. The successful raid and subsequent vassalage of Nicolás Maduro’s successor had Trump feeling very bullish about his vision of a Western Hemispheric empire. When people, including many in his own party, told him he was not allowed to have Greenland, it threatened his claim on absolute authority. Trump responded by pushing back until people begged him not to act, which is in itself an acknowledgement of his power. 

So what’s the next Greenland? Having to back down on this one will increase Trump’s need to show he remains a credible threat. 

It could be American banks, which the president is starting to slap around pretty good. And the fact that it was financial markets that knocked the legs out from Trump’s Greenland move will no doubt heighten the president’s interest in showing the bankers who’s boss. There’s Trump’s effort to get the Federal Reserve under his thumb, then there was the demand that banks cap credit card interest rates at an impossible 10 percent, and now a $5 billion lawsuit against JPMorgan Chase and its CEO, Jamie Dimon, for dumping Trump’s accounts after the then-president tried to overturn the results of the 2020 election. Banks have a lot more leverage than the media companies Trump squeezed at the start of last year, and the president’s demands are far more consequential than TV lineups. If the Supreme Court turns Trump away on the Fed as expected, the banks refuse to lose money on credit cards and Dimon tells Trump to go soak his head, do we get another round of brinkmanship and anxiety? 

Certainly, we’re going to see some Greenlanding on the 2026 election itself. Jack Smith’s blistering House testimony may bring that one to the top of the list soon. The president is promising criminal prosecutions of the people he claims stole the 2020 election from him, so far to no avail. And what about the Republicans who have refused to comply with demands for gerrymandering or to abolish mail-in and early voting? The closer the president gets to what looks like a November repudiation, we would reasonably expect him to turn up the heat. 

Or how about if the high court tells the president he lacks the power to set arbitrary tariffs? Or if the justices rule an eventual invocation of the Insurrection Act as out of bounds? Or if Bad Bunny trash talks Trump at the Super Bowl?  

Who knows which provocations — or perceived provocations — could trigger the next cycle of escalation. Trump himself probably doesn’t know right now, but will go with his gut and take his party along for the ride. It will all depend on how good or bad he is feeling at any given moment and what his pride and showman’s intuition tell him. 

While the world was watching Trump repel down the alpine heights of his Greenland claims in Davos, Switzerland, something quite unexpected was happening on Capitol Hill.  

Nine Democrats on the House Oversight and Government Reform Committee voted with their Republican counterparts to hold former President Bill Clinton in contempt for his refusal to comply with a subpoena to testify about his relationship with the infamous pedophile Jeffrey Epstein. Clinton has no actual power, and whatever soft power he enjoyed in the party was mostly drained by his wife’s two failed presidential runs and the post-facto #MeToo view of the then-president’s assignations with a 21-year-old White House intern. But even so, contempt of Congress for a former president of one’s own party in connection to a sex crimes investigation is really something. 

The source of most power in Congress comes from party unity, so throwing the former president to the wolves like that tells us a lot about Democrats’ fervor to make Trump pay for his own efforts to hide his long relationship with Epstein. Being the tough-on-sex-trafficking party is just good politics, especially when the other guys keep stumbling on the issue.  

Then note the declaration from 2028 Democratic presidential contender, former Chicago Mayor Rahm Emanuel, that he wants to impose an age limit of 75 on the presidency, the executive branch, the federal courts and Congress. Emanuel is a long shot for his party’s nomination, but he’s definitely on to some ideas that will get into the Democrats’ discussion. The best part of Emanuel’s pitch: He said it would apply to him if he were to become president and would limit the 66-year-old to one term.  

Or how about Sen. Elissa Slotkin (D-Mich.) and the pitch for a constitutional amendment to end presidential pardon powers, an idea that gains increasing traction as the current president’s pardon spree of politically connected fraudsters marches on.  

The theme here is a powerful one in American politics throughout the years: reform. 

In and after the first Trump term, Democrats settled on a different one-word concept for their party: democracy, which is as vague as reform but not nearly as satisfying. The ill-conceived and poorly executed election overhaul Democrats offered at the beginning of Joe Biden’s presidency was indicative of the larger problem. Getting people to care deeply about democracy in the abstract, especially on items seen as favorable to your own party’s chances, seems unlikely. 

Getting people to care about corruption, ethics and the abuse of power, on the other hand, is pretty straightforward. After electing the three oldest people to the presidency in a row and watching Congress remain unable to actually ban investment shenanigans by its members and those in the executive branch, there’s an appetite for reform. As Trump padded his net worth by some $1.5 billion in his first year in office while he gilts the White House and builds a ballroom, there’s probably even a demand. 

The “democracy” vibe was appealing to Democrats for many reasons, particularly the strong public backlash against Trump’s efforts to swipe a second term. But it also hearkened to their 2016 claims about Russian interference being to blame for Hillary Clinton’s loss. It was too much about them and their grievances. Cleaning house, especially when some of your own dust bunnies are getting swept up, is far more attractive to normal voters. 

Given the wild swings ahead for Trump and the Republicans this year, reform is the kind of simple message that could carry Democrats to a midterm landslide. 

[Programming alert: Watch “The Hill Sunday” with Chris Stirewalt — Who needs Greenland when you have this much winter weather headed your way? As much of the country hunkers down for a big snowstorm, Congress is following suit. We’ll talk to members on both sides of the aisle, including Rep. Mike Lawler (R-N.Y.), on the economy, ICE protests, and what exactly is going on in the talks with Denmark. Plus, we’ll continue our march toward America’s 250th birthday with revered American historian Gordon Wood. And, as always, we’ll have expert analysis from our best-in-the-business panel of journalists. Be sure to catch us on NewsNation at 10 a.m. ET / 9 a.m. CT or your local CW station.] 

Holy croakano! We welcome your feedback, so please email us with your tips, corrections, reactions, amplifications, etc. at WholeHogPolitics@TheHill.com. If you’d like to be considered for publication, please include your real name and hometown. If you don’t want your comments to be made public, please specify. 

NUTRITIONAL INFORMATION  

Trump Job Performance 

Average Approval: 40.2 percent 

Average Disapproval: 56 percent 

Net Score: -15.8 points 

Change from one week ago: ↑ 3.2 points (-19 points) 

Change from five weeks ago: ↓  1.6 points (-14.2 points) 

[Average includes: American Research Group 35% approve – 63% disapprove; Emerson College 43% approve – 51% disapprove; New York Times/Siena 40% approve – 56% disapprove; Wall Street Journal 45% approve – 54% disapprove; Marist 38% approve – 56% disapprove] 

Trump approval on par with recent midterm history 

Trump job approval, January 2018 

Overall: 37% 

Democrats: 7% 

Independents: 38% 

Republicans: 85% 

[Marist College, Jan. 8-10, 2018] 

Biden job approval, January 2022 

Overall: 40% 

Democrats: 82% 

Independents: 33% 

Republicans: 5% 

[Gallup, Jan. 3-16, 2022]  

Trump job approval, January 2026 

Overall: 40% 

Democrats: 4% 

Independents: 34% 

Republicans: 86% 

[New York Times/Siena, Jan. 12-17] 

ON THE SIDE: LEAVING LAS VEGAS  

Writer Jasper Craven heads to Sin City for a look at the past and future of gambling as America goes all in on sports betting. Harper’s: “Just through the front doors of [the International Gaming Institute at the University of Nevada, Las Vegas] is the Gaming Lab, where experts attempt to divine the future of gambling. There I met the center’s director of gaming innovation, Daniel Sahl, who told me he had come to believe that we are headed toward a more streamlined version of what he called ‘meaningful winning experiences.’ The game he proceeded to show me on his screen was not a fancy, flashy prototype but a simple black line moving upward. The winner is the person who can hold on to this trajectory the longest, ideally cashing out in the microsecond before the randomized formula shoots the line perilously downward. Depending on your vantage point, the game either mimics an accelerated version of the stock market or a human heartbeat on a medical monitor. It’s called Crash, and I bet that one day you’ll be playing it.” 

SHORT ORDER 

Department of Government Efficiency staffer shared private Social Security information as part of effort to overturn election results — Washington Post 

Klobuchar files initial paperwork for Minnesota gubernatorial run — The Hill 

Former NFL sideline reporter Michele Tafoya opens bid for Minnesota Senate as a Republican — The Hill  

Rep. Jasmine Crockett (D-Texas) and supporters push back hard on Democratic criticisms of her Senate candidacy — Washington Post 

Nate Silver debuts 2026 generic ballot average with Dems up 5.3 points — Silver Bulletin 

TABLE TALK: VERY ONLINE  

“Quite frankly, I don’t give a damn what these libtards and these people that want to criticize, quite honestly, the more they criticize me and our team and my family, we’re doing good work.” — Nebraska Gov. Jim Pillen (R) in a tele-townhall pitching his legislative agenda. 

MAILBAG 

“I generally agree with your outlook that, despite the ‘bad’ Senate map, Democrats can possibly win a majority in that body in this year’s election. It’ll take a Democratic breeze and winnable primary candidates, but I think it’s possible. Democrats need to hold such contestable seats as the ones in Georgia, Michigan, New Hampshire and Minnesota and to win enough seats currently held by Republicans in North Carolina, Maine, Ohio, Alaska, Iowa and Texas that there is a gain of four seats for the party.  Holding Minnesota should be easy, while winning Texas and/or Iowa is likely to be very tough. … It’s going to take a lot of contributions from Democrats in and out of those states to achieve a Democratic Senate majority in 2027 but it shouldn’t be seen as impossible.” — Ken Stevens, Columbia, Md. 

Mr. Stevens, 

I think Democrats need to understand that Georgia and Michigan are very different from New Hampshire and Minnesota. The latter two are reaches for Republicans.  

You should email us! Write to WholeHogPolitics@TheHill.com with your tips, kudos, criticisms, insights, rediscovered words, wonderful names, recipes, and, always, good jokes. Please include your real name — at least first and last — and hometown. Make sure to let us know in the email if you want to keep your submission private. My colleague, Meera Sehgal, and I will look for your emails and then share the most interesting ones and my responses here. Clickety clack! 

FOR DESSERT: 21 JUMP STREET  

KSTU: “An artificial intelligence that writes police reports had some explaining to do earlier this month after it claimed a Heber City [Utah] officer had shape-shifted into a frog. … ‘The body cam software and the AI report writing software picked up on the movie that was playing in the background, which happened to be ‘The Princess and the Frog,’’ Sgt. Rick Keel [said]. ‘That’s when we learned the importance of correcting these AI-generated reports.’ Earlier this month, the department began testing two pieces of AI software … in hopes of reducing paperwork and allowing officers to be out in the field more. … Keel says one of the major draws is that the software saves them time, as writing reports typically takes 1-2 hours. ‘I’m saving myself about 6-8 hours weekly now,’ Keel said. ‘I’m not the most tech-savvy person, so it’s very user-friendly.’”

data.title: Trump's ICE invasion is a slippery slope America may not survive
data.description:
I never imagined I would need to learn what my legal responsibilities were if federal law enforcement showed up at my workplace. I never thought I might have to ask to see a search warrant.

I’m a business owner in Minneapolis. I employ hundreds of people. I pay my taxes, and I don’t commit crimes. I try, in all the boring and unglamorous ways, to be a model citizen. Until recently, that felt sufficient. 

But there is something different in the air here right now. The anger is palpable, and it isn’t confined to one side. Both sides are hardening in real time. People are no longer arguing to persuade; they’re bracing for impact. Beneath the anger is something quieter and more corrosive: fear. 

I employ many Hispanic workers — all legal. Some have been with me for more than 15 years. They show up every day, do difficult work, and quietly build lives for their families. To me, they represent the American dream as much as anyone. Many came from places where stability was fragile and safety was never guaranteed. That history matters, because fear has a memory. 

I now see that fear in their eyes. 

I asked one employee how worried he is, on a scale of zero to 100. He said 50. Then he said something that stopped me cold. “It’s in God’s hands now.” That wasn’t theology; it was resignation. It was someone placing his safety outside the reach of human institutions. 

Another employee asked if he could sleep in the office rather than drive home if he ever felt uncomfortable. He was afraid of being caught in the wrong place at the wrong time. I told him of course. The idea that someone would sleep at work out of fear of being profiled was hard to process.

This is not how a healthy society feels. 

One day, an ICE agent pulled into our parking lot, near an entrance where trucks come in and out. I went outside to speak with him. I had no intention of being confrontational; I simply wanted to understand why he was there and to make sure nothing interfered with operations. 

The agent did nothing wrong. He didn’t threaten anyone. He didn’t demand anything, and he left shortly after. There was no misconduct, no abuse of authority, no dramatic confrontation. 

And yet, as I walked toward him, I felt something new. 

When I was a child, at a Passover dinner, I once asked what the numbers tattooed on someone’s forearm meant. The room went silent. Faces changed. There was panic in the air, not because I had done something wrong, but because the adults suddenly had to explain something they weren’t prepared to explain to a child. That moment stayed with me. And as I approached that ICE vehicle, that memory surfaced.  

I am not naive about the law. Being in this country illegally is a violation. A society cannot function if it simply ignores its own rules. Enforcement matters, borders matter, and law matters. But when tension is this high, the margin for error collapses. A single mistake can have consequences that no policy memo or talking point can undo. That is what frightens me. 

We have done this before. During the Obama years, enforcement was real, but it was carried out with targeting, warrants and an intentional absence of public spectacle. Fear did not radiate outward from it. 

Why does it require 3,000 ICE officers in a relatively small metropolitan area with covered faces? When enforcement becomes this expansive, it stops drawing clear lines between threat and bystander. And when those lines blur, fear fills the space they leave behind. 

What pushed me from concern into disbelief came when Vice President JD Vance said in a televised interview the government would go “door to door.” I found that phrase incomprehensible. “Door to door” is not legal language. It is not constitutional language. But it is emotional language — the language of fear.

The U.S. has a Constitution for a reason. It exists precisely to restrain power when emotions run high. I never wanted to be in a position where I had to consider asking an officer of the law to show me a warrant. I never imagined I would think about my workplace as a potential refuge. These are not thoughts that belong in the mind of someone who has done nothing wrong.

And yet they are here. 

I still believe there is hope. We can enforce the law without humiliating the innocent. We can secure borders without destabilizing communities. We can act firmly without acting recklessly. But we have to recognize how close we are to something going terribly wrong. 

That is not a political warning. It is a moral one. 

Corey Kvasnick is an entrepreneur, investor, philanthropist, and a contributor to Common Ground Thinking.
